content:
{
  "title": "Contextual Adversarial Masked Prompting: Preventing Jailbreaks via Dynamic Prompt Obfuscation",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to jailbreak and adversarial attacks that manipulate prompts to elicit undesirable outputs. Existing defenses largely rely on static prompt engineering or adaptive guardrails, which may be circumvented by sophisticated attacks.",
  "motivation": "As attackers rapidly develop prompt-based exploits, static or template-based defenses cannot keep up with the evolving adversarial landscape. There is an urgent need for prompting techniques that dynamically adapt at inference time, leveraging LLMs’ contextual understanding to obscure sensitive instructions without degrading utility. Such methods can provide robust, model-agnostic protection without costly retraining.",
  "method": "We propose Contextual Adversarial Masked Prompting (CAMP), a prompting framework that dynamically masks or obfuscates critical intent tokens and instructions within the original prompt using context-aware paraphrasing and controlled token substitution. CAMP leverages a lightweight pre-processor LLM to identify high-risk prompt regions, then applies selective masking or context-preserving paraphrases, yielding a transformed prompt that maintains utility for benign users but disrupts adversarial pattern matching or instruction leakage. The obfuscation is randomized per session, impeding prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, and prompt-leaking attacks.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CAMP on a suite of jailbreak and adversarial prompt benchmarks such as JailbreakBench and PLeak, as well as newly curated attacks. Baselines include AdaShield, static paraphrasing, and standard prompting. Metrics include attack success rate, task accuracy, and utility preservation for benign prompts."
}
